title: Physics-Transfer Learning: A Framework to Address the Accuracy-Performance Dilemma in Modeling Complexity Problems in Engineering Sciences

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a physics-transfer (PT) learning framework that learns mappings between physically meaningful descriptors and target quantities from low-fidelity/simpler digital models, and then performs zero-shot extrapolation by feeding high-fidelity inputs into the learned mapping. Two case studies are presented: (i) predicting Peierls stress for metals by training on MD/EAM or MLFF data and then inferring at DFT-level accuracy from DFT-derived elastic constants and γ-surfaces; and (ii) predicting brain morphology development by learning bifurcation-relevant features on spheres/ellipsoids and extrapolating to human brain MRI data using a GNN. The authors argue this approach addresses the accuracy–performance dilemma by retaining high-fidelity accuracy at the inference cost of a surrogate.
strengths: The paper articulates a clear and generally applicable idea: learn x→O mappings that represent invariant “physics,” then swap in high-fidelity x at inference time to approximate high-fidelity O without high-fidelity labels.

The materials case leverages a physically motivated feature set (elastic constants and γ-surfaces) and an architecture (CNN+FNN) aligned with that physics; the morphology case uses geometric GNNs and normal-based constraints to encode bifurcation-relevant cues.
The notion of aligning parameter posteriors p(θ|D′) across fidelities when the same physics is captured is conceptually appealing and aligned with multi-fidelity learning goals.
Two disparate domains (materials and biomechanics) are covered, suggesting generality beyond a single application niche.
For materials, comparisons are made across EAM, MLFF, and DFT; small and large simulation scales are considered; computational cost vs accuracy trade-offs are documented.
The study demonstrates a screening pipeline over 3,471 metastable crystals (from GNoME) and shows identified strong candidates match known high-yield-strength elements.
The motivation and the “accuracy–performance dilemma” are clearly framed; figures and schematics convey the main idea well.
Engineering details for simulations and models are provided in the Appendix, with concrete hyperparameters and numerical setups.
If made rigorous, PT could reduce the need for high-fidelity labels in non-equilibrium problems where such labels are impractical, enabling higher-throughput discovery workflows.
The approach could generalize to other multi-fidelity scientific modeling tasks with physically structured feature spaces.
weaknesses: The formalism is largely conceptual; there is no theoretical characterization of when p(θ|D′LF)≈p(θ|D′HF) holds, what D′ must contain, or how to detect when the assumption breaks.

The claim that PT “eliminates physical and system uncertainties” is overstated; at best it sidesteps some sources by substituting high-fidelity inputs but introduces others (e.g., representation error, distribution shift).

Uncertainty quantification and its decomposition are not methodologically defined; “percent contributions” appear to be computed from inter-method discrepancies without a principled probabilistic model.

Materials: key evaluation details are missing (dataset sizes, train/val/test splits, cross-validation strategy, per-composition holdout). The largest headline error “below 48.91%” is high for a central claim of “chemically accurate.”
MLFF is used as “ground truth” for large systems despite known limitations for dislocation cores; this risks circular validation and weakens the evidence for accuracy at scale.

Brain morphology: quantitative metrics are absent (e.g., surface-to-surface distances, curvature distribution distances, gyrification index error, geodesic or Hausdorff distances, longitudinal consistency). Results appear largely qualitative or single-curve comparisons.
The screening study showcases known strong metals (Os, Ru, Re, Tc); evidence of novel, non-trivial discoveries or prospective validation is limited.
The precise training data counts, coverage over chemistries/slip systems, and the number of DFT-derived γ-surfaces used for PT inference are unclear.
It is not specified how γ-surfaces are encoded for CNN input (grid resolution standardization, alignment across slip systems/lattices, normalization).
In the brain section, the definition and scaling of the time-like parameter t and how it maps to longitudinal MRI time points are not adequately described.

The discussion of multi-fidelity learning omits classical co-kriging and Gaussian process co-surrogates, domain adaptation under covariate shift, physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), operator learning (e.g., DeepONet, FNO), and fidelity-aware calibration approaches.
Comparisons to Δ-learning and LFAF are mostly conceptual; no experimental baselines are reported for these methods (or physics-informed variants) on the authors’ tasks.
reviews.questions:
1. How large are the training datasets (number of samples per lattice and slip system) and how are train/validation/test splits constructed to avoid leakage across near-duplicate chemistries or potentials?
2. What exact preprocessing is applied to γ-surfaces for CNN input (grid size, coordinate alignment across slip systems, normalization), and how sensitive are results to these choices?
3. Can you provide quantitative metrics (MAE/RMSE/R2) over all metals/slip systems and learning curves to support the “chemically accurate” claim, given that some reported relative errors are as high as ~49%?
4. How is the uncertainty decomposition in Fig. 2i–j computed? Is there a probabilistic model underlying the percentages, and how would the decomposition change if the MLFF ground truth is biased?
5. For the screening study, what is the total DFT cost per material to obtain elastic constants and reconstruct γ-surfaces from “a few single-point calculations”? Please quantify screening throughput versus computing budget.
6. Did you evaluate PT on holdout chemistries or entirely unseen slip systems to assess extrapolation beyond interpolation across the training manifold?
7. For the brain experiments, how many MRI subjects and timepoints are used, and what quantitative metrics (e.g., curvature distribution distance, gyrification index error, surface distances) do you achieve compared to baselines?
8. What is the semantics and timescale of the parameter t in the brain plots? How is it aligned to gestational or postnatal age in the MRI data?
9. Have you compared PT to Δ-learning/LFAF or physics-informed baselines on your materials task using a small set of DFT Peierls labels to validate the advantage when limited high-fidelity labels are available?
10. Can you provide an OOD or domain-shift diagnostic that detects when p(xHF) falls outside the low-fidelity training support, to avoid unreliable zero-shot extrapolations?
reviews.assessment: This paper presents an attractive and practically motivated idea with cross-domain demonstrations: learning invariant, physically grounded mappings on low-fidelity data and swapping in high-fidelity inputs at inference to approximate high-fidelity targets. The two case studies convincingly illustrate potential, and the engineering is thoughtful and relevant. However, the methodological core is largely conceptual, with limited formalization or mechanisms to ensure cross-fidelity invariance. Empirical support is mixed: materials results rely partly on MLFF as ground truth for large systems and include sizable errors; brain results are largely qualitative without standard quantitative metrics or rigorous baselines. Uncertainty quantification is not methodologically well-founded. The contribution would be significantly strengthened by (i) clear experimental protocols and metrics, (ii) comparisons to established multi-fidelity and physics-informed baselines, (iii) principled UQ, and (iv) quantitative brain evaluation. As it stands, the work is promising but not yet at the level of rigor and validation expected for ICLR. I encourage the authors to tighten the evaluation and provide stronger empirical and methodological foundations.
